subject: First Year Analysis
instructions: Answer FOUR questions in detail. State carefully any results used without proof.

problems.text: The function \(f:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}\) is Riemann-integrable and vanishes at each point of a dense set. Does it follow that \(\int_0^1 f=0\)? Proof or counterexample, as appropriate.
Let \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) be continuous. Prove that there exists a sequence \((p_n)\) of polynomials that converges to \(f\) both

subparts.label: (ii)
subparts.text: uniformly on each compact set in \(\mathbb{R}\) (simultaneously).

problems.text: Let \(\mathcal{F}\subseteq C(X)\) be equicontinuous and let \(B\) be the set comprising all points of \(X\) at which \(\mathcal{F}\) is bounded; that is, \(a\in B\) exactly when there exists \(K\) such that \(|f(a)|<K\) whenever \(f\in\mathcal{F}\). Prove that \(B\subseteq X\) is both closed and open.
Let \((f_n)\) be a sequence of measurable real-valued functions on \(\Omega\). Prove that each of the following subsets of \(\Omega\) is measurable:

Let \((f_n)\) be a sequence of bounded, measurable, real-valued functions on \(\Omega\) and assume that \(f_n\to f\) uniformly. Prove that if \(\mu(\Omega)<\infty\) then
\[
\int_\Omega f_n\,d\mu\to\int_\Omega f\,d\mu
\]
and show by example that this can fail if \(\mu(\Omega)=\infty\).
